---
title: "The Model DSL"
---

## Overview {#sec-model-dsl}

ferx models live in plain-text `.ferx` files.
Each file has up to seven sections in a fixed order.
Three sections are required; the rest have sensible defaults.

| Section | Required | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `[parameters]` | Yes | Declare thetas, omegas, sigmas with bounds |
| `[individual_parameters]` | Yes | Express individual PK params in terms of thetas + ETAs |
| `[structural_model]` | Yes | Analytical PK shorthand or ODE declaration |
| `[odes]` | If ODE model | Differential equations (one per state) |
| `[error_model]` | Yes | Residual error distribution |
| `[initial_values]` | No | Override starting values without editing the bound lines |
| `[fit_options]` | No | Estimation method, max iterations, covariance |

## `[parameters]`

Thetas use the form `theta NAME(typical, lower, upper)`.
The three numbers are: typical value (starting estimate), lower bound, upper bound.

```
[parameters]
  theta TVCL(0.134, 0.001, 10.0)   # CL typical value, bounded (0.001, 10)
  theta TVV(8.1,   0.1,   500.0)
  theta TVKA(1.0,  0.01,  50.0)
```

Omegas declare between-subject variability:

```
  omega ETA_CL ~ 0.07    # variance (not SD) — initial estimate
  omega ETA_V  ~ 0.02
  omega ETA_KA ~ 0.40
```

The `~` syntax means "variance of this ETA".
ferx always treats the omega matrix as diagonal unless you use the block syntax (see @sec-iov).

Sigma declares residual variance:

```
  sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.01   # proportional error variance
```

The optional `(sd)` qualifier lets you specify the initial value on the
**standard-deviation** scale instead of variance — usually more intuitive for
sigmas and IIV alike:

```
  sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.10 (sd)     # 10% on the SD scale -> var = 0.01
  omega  ETA_CL  ~ 0.30 (sd)     # 30% CV on the log scale
```

The estimate, the `init_as_sd` column in `ferx_estimates()`, and the printed
output all preserve this distinction so users always know which scale the
initial value was declared on.

### Block / correlated random effects

For correlated ETAs (or kappas), use the block form with the column-major
lower-triangle of the covariance matrix:

```
[parameters]
  block_omega (ETA_CL, ETA_V) = [0.09, 0.02, 0.04]   # var(CL), cov(CL,V), var(V)
  block_kappa (KAPPA_CL, KAPPA_V) = [0.02, 0.005, 0.01]
```

The three values for a 2×2 block are `[var1, cov12, var2]` — column-major
lower triangle (subscripts: row, col). For a 3×3 block the six values are
`[var1, cov21, cov31, var2, cov32, var3]`, where `cov_ij` means row=i, col=j.

Use a block omega when CL and V are expected to co-vary (e.g. allometric
subjects with both higher CL and larger V). A positive off-diagonal `cov12`
means subjects with higher-than-typical CL also tend to have higher-than-typical
V. The diagonal-only default (`omega ETA_CL ~ 0.09`) assumes independence.

`ferx_cor_matrix(fit)` reports the estimated correlation matrix for a block
omega after fitting. The bundled `warfarin_block_omega` example demonstrates
this; see `ferx_example("warfarin_block_omega")`.

### `kappa` — inter-occasion variability

The `kappa` keyword declares an occasion-level random effect; see @sec-iov.

### Fixing parameters (`FIX`) {#sec-fix}

Append `FIX` to any `theta`, `omega`, `sigma`, or `kappa` declaration to hold
the parameter at its initial value during estimation:

```
[parameters]
  theta TVCL(0.134, 0.001, 10.0)
  theta TVLAG(0.25, 0.0, 2.0) FIX   # lag time fixed at 0.25
  omega ETA_CL ~ 0.07
  omega ETA_KA ~ 0.40 FIX            # BSV on KA fixed
  sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.01 (sd) FIX     # sigma fixed on SD scale
  kappa KAPPA_CL ~ 0.05 FIX          # IOV variance fixed
```

**Placement is flexible.** `FIX` may appear at the end of the line (after the
closing parenthesis) or, for theta, inside the parentheses:

```
theta TVLAG(0.25, 0.0, 2.0) FIX   # end of line — traditional NONMEM style
theta TVLAG(0.25, FIX)             # two-element form: init + FIX, no bounds
theta CL (FIX) (0.1, 0.001, 10.0) # FIX anywhere before the bound triple
omega ETA_CL (FIX) ~ 0.07         # FIX before the tilde
```

Fixed parameters appear in `print(fit)` and `ferx_estimates()` with
`SE = FIXED` and `%RSE = FIXED`; they are excluded from the covariance matrix.

**Theta bounds.** The full form is `(initial, lower, upper)`.
You may omit the upper bound when there is no natural ceiling:

```
theta CL(0.134, 0.001, 10.0)   # three-element: init, lower, upper
theta CL(0.134, 0.001)         # lower-bound only; no upper bound
```

**Unused-parameter warning.** Declaring a parameter in `[parameters]` but not
referencing it in `[individual_parameters]` or `[error_model]` triggers a
`warning`-severity message with category `"unused_parameter"`.  This usually
indicates a commented-out expression or a typo.  Inspect `ferx_warnings(fit)` or
run `ferx_model_validate()` before fitting.

## `[individual_parameters]`

Each line assigns an individual PK parameter using thetas and ETAs.

```
[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)   # log-normal BSV
  V  = TVV  * exp(ETA_V)
  KA = TVKA * exp(ETA_KA)
```

Any expression valid in R-like syntax is accepted: arithmetic, `exp()`, `log()`, `inv_logit()`, `logit()`, covariate columns from the dataset.

### Covariate effects

Use dataset column names directly in expressions:

```
[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * (WT / 70)^THETA_WT * (CRCL / 100)^THETA_CRCL * exp(ETA_CL)
  V1 = TVV1 * (WT / 70)^THETA_WT * exp(ETA_V1)
```

`WT` and `CRCL` are read from the dataset row for each subject.
The power-model form `(cov / ref)^theta` is the conventional allometric scaling.

### Conditional logic (`if` / `else`)

`[individual_parameters]` supports `if`/`else` for covariate-driven branching.

**Block form** — use when the body contains multiple assignments or more than
two branches:

```
[individual_parameters]
  if (WT > 70) {
    CL = TVCL * (WT / 70)^0.75 * exp(ETA_CL)
  } else {
    CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)
  }
  V = TVV * exp(ETA_V)
```

**Inline (ternary) form** — use for a single-value branch:

```
[individual_parameters]
  CL = if (SEX == 1) TVCL * 1.5 * exp(ETA_CL) else TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)
  V  = TVV * exp(ETA_V)
```

Conditions support `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`, `&&`, `||`, `!`.
Both forms can be combined freely in the same block.

Note: ferx skips mu-reference detection for parameters assigned inside an
`if` block, since the ETA→THETA relationship is not unconditional there.
Unconditional assignments in the same block are unaffected.

The bundled `warfarin_if` example demonstrates a two-branch allometric CL
model; see `ferx_example("warfarin_if")`.

## `[structural_model]`

### Analytical PK shortcuts

ferx provides built-in analytical solutions for common compartment models:

| Keyword | Cmts | Route | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| `one_cpt_oral(cl, v, ka)` | 1 | Oral | `cl`, `v`, `ka` |
| `one_cpt_iv_bolus(cl, v)` | 1 | IV bolus | `cl`, `v` |
| `one_cpt_infusion(cl, v)` | 1 | IV infusion | `cl`, `v` |
| `two_cpt_oral(cl, v1, q, v2, ka)` | 2 | Oral | `cl`, `v1`, `q`, `v2`, `ka` |
| `two_cpt_iv_bolus(cl, v1, q, v2)` | 2 | IV bolus | `cl`, `v1`, `q`, `v2` |
| `two_cpt_infusion(cl, v1, q, v2)` | 2 | IV infusion | `cl`, `v1`, `q`, `v2` |
| `three_cpt_oral(cl, v1, q2, v2, q3, v3, ka)` | 3 | Oral | `cl`, `v1`, `q2`, `v2`, `q3`, `v3`, `ka` |
| `three_cpt_iv_bolus(cl, v1, q2, v2, q3, v3)` | 3 | IV bolus | `cl`, `v1`, `q2`, `v2`, `q3`, `v3` |
| `three_cpt_infusion(cl, v1, q2, v2, q3, v3)` | 3 | IV infusion | `cl`, `v1`, `q2`, `v2`, `q3`, `v3` |

All analytical models accept an optional `lagtime=` (or `alag=`) argument; oral models additionally accept an optional `f=` bioavailability argument. See @sec-lagtime and @sec-bioavailability.

These are invoked with named arguments:

```
[structural_model]
  pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)
```

The `pk` keyword tells the parser to use the analytical solution engine.
See the [ferx-core PK models](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/pk-models.html) reference for derivations.

### ODE models

When no analytical solution exists, declare `ode(...)` and write ODEs in `[odes]`:

```
[structural_model]
  ode(obs_cmt=central, states=[depot, central])

[odes]
  d/dt(depot)   = -KA * depot
  d/dt(central) = KA * depot / V - VMAX * central / (KM + central)
```

`obs_cmt` names the compartment whose amount is used for the `DV` prediction.
See @sec-ode-models for a full ODE example.

## `[error_model]`

```
[error_model]
  DV ~ proportional(PROP_ERR)
```

Supported error distributions:

| Syntax | Model | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| `proportional(sigma)` | `DV = IPRED * (1 + eps)` | Concentration data, relative variability |
| `additive(sigma)` | `DV = IPRED + eps` | Log-scale or count data |
| `combined(sigma_prop, sigma_add)` | Both | Transitional concentration ranges |
| `log_additive(sigma)` | Additive on log scale; DV already log-transformed | Importing pre-logged data |

`eps` is drawn from `N(0, sigma)` for each observation. All sigma parameters
are on the standard-deviation scale.

For log-transform-both-sides (LTBS) with DV on the **natural scale**, use the
alternative declaration `log(DV) ~ additive(sigma)` — the engine log-transforms
the DV column at load time. Use `DV ~ log_additive(sigma)` when the dataset
already contains log-transformed values. See @sec-ltbs for full details.

For multi-endpoint models (simultaneous PK/PD), prefix each line with `CMT=N:`
to assign a distinct error model per compartment:

```
[error_model]
  CMT=2: DV ~ proportional(PROP_ERR_PK)
  CMT=3: DV ~ additive(ADD_ERR_PD)
```

See @sec-ltbs for LTBS details and @sec-multi-endpoint for per-CMT error models.

## `[initial_values]`

The `[initial_values]` section lets you override starting estimates without touching the bound lines.
This is convenient for re-running after a first fit:

```
[initial_values]
  theta = [0.133, 7.89, 1.49]
  omega = [0.0425, 0.0162, 0.328]
  sigma = [0.0162]
```

Values must be listed in the same order the parameters appear in `[parameters]`.

## `[fit_options]`

```
[fit_options]
  method     = focei    # foce | focei | saem | gn | gn_hybrid
  maxiter    = 300
  covariance = true
```

Omit this section to use the defaults (`method = foce`, `maxiter = 300`, `covariance = false`).

## Optional blocks

A complete reference of advanced blocks is in @sec-special-features; the
short form:

| Block | Purpose | Chapter |
|---|---|---|
| `[scaling]` | Unit conversion (`obs_scale`) and per-CMT readouts (`y[CMT=N] = ...`) | @sec-special-features |
| `[diffusion]` | Stochastic differential equations (process noise) | @sec-sde |
| `[covariate_nn NAME]` | Neural-network covariate effect (Deep Compartment Models) | — (advanced) |
| `[simulation]` | Embedded simulation trial design | @sec-simulation |

## Validating a model

Before running a long fit, check syntax:

```{r}
#| label: validate
#| message: false
library(ferx)
ferx_model_validate(ferx_example("warfarin")$model)
```

If a section is missing or malformed, the validator reports which section
failed and `Result: INVALID`. The return value is `TRUE` / `FALSE`, so the
function can be used programmatically.
